title: Raised-cord binding
subtitle: Sewing signatures over hemp cords to produce visible ridge bands on the spine
excerpt: A raised-cord binding is the historical case-bound form, visible on any old hardback where the spine shows five or six horizontal ridges under the leather or cloth. The signatures sew over real cords, and those cords press ridges into the spine covering. This is Cockerell's method.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on sewing on cords and the raised-cord book structure. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. The five-cord layout, spine lining, and head-cap shaping described here follow Cockerell's canonical specification.

content.text: Every old hardback you open with raised bands on the spine was sewn on
raised cords

. The cords run across the spine at fixed intervals; the thread from each signature loops over each cord as it passes. The result is a firm, flexible text block with the cords projecting as tabs at the spine edge; those tab ends are pushed through the boards so the boards become physically anchored to the text block, not just glued. This is Cockerell's canonical five-cord structure for a medium-format journal.
What you need

items.name: Text paper, 90 to 130 gsm, grain-long, cut to A5

items.qty: 5 lengths cut 70 mm wider than the text block
items.name: Hemp or linen cord, 3 to 4 mm diameter

items.qty: Approximately 3 times the height of all signatures combined
items.name: Bookbinding thread, waxed linen, 4-cord
Binders board, 3 mm, cut 3 mm larger than text block at head, tail, and foredge

items.qty: Enough for full-cloth cover with turn-ins
items.name: Book cloth

items.qty: Cut to spine width plus 30 mm each side
items.name: Mull or crash (open-weave cotton fabric)
Bone folder, cutting mat, metal ruler, craft knife

attrs.body: This binding needs confident sewing on tapes. Work through sewing-on-tapes and case-binding-introduction first. The Cockerell case-binding method is the base.
attrs.heading: Advanced binding

content.text: Preparing the sewing frame and signatures

Attach five cords to the sewing frame, spaced evenly between 20 mm from the head and 20 mm from the tail. Mark the corresponding sewing stations on the piercing template: one station on each side of each cord, plus a head station and a tail station. Fold and pierce all signatures using the template.
Sewing on cords

Begin with the first endpaper signature. Sew the thread path from head to tail, looping over each cord as you cross it: exit at the station before the cord, loop the thread over and under the cord, re-enter at the same hole, then exit at the station after the cord and continue.
At the tail station of the first signature, hold the tail of the thread while you add the second signature. Sew back from tail to head, working the
kettle stitch
at the head and tail of each signature from the second onward.
Add all text signatures, then the back endpaper. Tie off at the tail of the final signature. Remove from the sewing frame by cutting the cords to leave 35 mm projecting from each side of the spine.
Consolidating the spine
Knock up the text block (tap the head and spine edges firmly on a flat surface to align everything square). Press between boards for 10 minutes.
Apply a thin coat of PVA to the spine, working between the cord ridges and along the head and tail. Lay the
spine lining
(mull or crash) over the wet spine, centred so it overlaps each side by 15 mm. Press it into the glue with a waste-paper pad. Allow to dry overnight under weight.
Lacing on the boards

Mark and pierce five pairs of channels through each board, corresponding to the five cord positions. The channels are angled: a hole piercing through the board from the spine edge, re-emerging on the inner face about 12 mm in.
Thread each cord through its corresponding channel and out the inner face. Fray the cord end and spread it flat against the inner face of the board. Paste it down with PVA.
Press both boards firmly against the spine with the lining flaps also pasted to the inner board face. Press under weight for several hours.
Covering and casing in

Cover the boards and spine with book cloth, taking care to press the cloth into each cord ridge with the bone folder as the cover dries. This presses the cloth down between the ridges so the cords read as raised bands through the cover, rather than lumps. Paste down the endpapers following the pasting-down tutorial.
Finishing and care
Press the finished book in the book press for 4 hours, working the
head cap
into shape with the bone folder while the PVA is still soft. The head cap should fold cleanly over the top cord and tuck into the joint. Store upright with support; the corded spine is very durable.
